In this chapter, God’s dealings with the Jewish nation and their behavior toward him are described, along with their punishment through surrounding nations, even those they most trusted. This is presented under the story of an infant rescued from death, educated, betrothed, and richly provided for, but later guilty of extremely reckless behavior and punished for it; yet finally received into favor and ashamed of her shameful conduct. We are not to judge these expressions by modern ideas, but by those of the times and places in which they were used, where many of them would not sound as they do to us. The purpose was to inspire hatred of idolatry, and such a story was well suited for that purpose.

After a full warning of judgments, mercy is remembered; mercy is reserved. These closing verses are a valuable promise, partly fulfilled at the return of the repentant and reformed Jews out of Babylon, but to have fuller accomplishment in gospel times. God’s mercy should be powerful enough to soften our hearts into godly sorrow for sin. Nor will God ever leave the sinner to perish who is humbled for his sins and comes to trust in His mercy and grace through Jesus Christ; but will keep him by His power, through faith unto salvation.
